coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the constituents of microwave-synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their effects on the photocatalytic performance of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) semiconductors. The synthesized g-C3N4 and CQDs were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), UV–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. The influence of CQD synthesis parameters on photoluminescence (PL) behavior was analyzed through time-integrated photoluminescence spectroscopy and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL). CQD purification via column chromatography and heat treatment enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/g-C3N4 composites, achieving up to 90 % methylene blue (MB) degradation. Extended CQDs pyrolysis resulted in fluorophore degradation and the formation of carbogenic cores, contributing to increased photocatalytic activity. Cytotoxicity testing confirmed the nontoxic nature of CQDs components and the composite. The high efficiency, low toxicity, and durability of the CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst present promising applications for renewable energy, environmental remediation, and sustainable chemical processes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the constituents of microwave-synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their effects on the photocatalytic performance of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) semiconductors. The synthesized g-C3N4 and CQDs were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), UV–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. The influence of CQD synthesis parameters on photoluminescence (PL) behavior was analyzed through time-integrated photoluminescence spectroscopy and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL). CQD purification via column chromatography and heat treatment enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/g-C3N4 composites, achieving up to 90 % methylene blue (MB) degradation. Extended CQDs pyrolysis resulted in fluorophore degradation and the formation of carbogenic cores, contributing to increased photocatalytic activity. Cytotoxicity testing confirmed the nontoxic nature of CQDs components and the composite. The high efficiency, low toxicity, and durability of the CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst present promising applications for renewable energy, environmental remediation, and sustainable chemical processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to their superior properties, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received increasing attention since their first discovery in 2004 [1]. Their distinctive features, such as non-toxicity, tunable luminescence, high stability, and ease of synthesis, make these carbon-based nanoparticles unique [2–4]. As a consequence, CQDs rose to prominence and have been widely adopted in various applications such as bioimaging [5], sensing [6], optoelectronic devices [7], catalysis [8], and photocatalysis [9].Generally, CQDs are synthesized by following the bottom-up approach, where citric acid and amino compounds are used as precursors, subjected to treatment under microwave radiation [10] or in a hydrothermal vessel [11], or in furnace for a longer duration [12]. When synthesized, CQDs are produced along with other species such as impurities, polymeric compounds, photoluminescent species and remnants of precursors [13–15]. Therefore, the produced solution of CQDs reflects the average properties of all species present in solution, and not solely CQDs nanoparticles. In the realm of catalysis, CQDs are still the subject of a heated debate. Indeed, most published studies report the direct utilization of CQDs either without the exclusion of impurities or with ambiguous separation procedures that lack a clear explanation regarding the nature of the separated components and the subsequent impact of purification on quantum yield (Φ) and catalytic activity. Hence, elucidating the nature of the components of CQDs solutions is a challenge requiring an attainable resolution.

Over the past few decades, a widespread consensus of environmental problems has emerged amid the increase of anthropogenic perturbations of natural ecosystems. Recently, there has been an unprecedented upswing in photocatalysis adoption for environment remediation [16]. Akin to natural photosynthesis, photocatalysis relies on sunlight to drive chemical reactions [17]. Due to its notable redox properties and sustainable nature, photocatalysis is a highly endorsed technique for the degradation of pollutants introduced in the biosphere [18,19]. Lately, graphitic carbon nitrides (g-C3N4) captivated scientists’ interest as an anticipated substitute for conventional metal-based photocatalysts [20,21]. This growing interest stems from g-C3N4 distinctive and tunable electronic and optical properties as well as from its nontoxicity, its ease of production, and the availability of precursors [22,23]. However, the low electron mobility in g-C3N4 hinders its efficiency and thus, thwarts its large-scale utilization [24–26]. To overcome this problem, researchers successfully incorporated metal and nonmetal elements on g-C3N4 sheets [27], and performed textural changes that targeted the improvement of the conductivity [28,29] and the augmentation of the surface area [30], respectively. Although doping g-C3N4 with noble metals appears to yield favourable results in increasing the material activity and durability [31,32], adopting noble metals-doped g-C3N4 on a large scale will greatly increase the operating cost, and therefore makes it financially unfeasible [33].

When produced via a bottom-up approach, a CQDs solution encompasses not only nanoscale carbon entities, but also a diverse array of byproducts, impurities, agglomerated species, and possibly, unreacted precursors. Therefore, the exclusion of these components is essential to preserve the catalytic activity of semiconductors into which CQDs will be incorporated. Recently, many studies suggested that the high fluorescence of CQDs solutions is attributed to the formation of fluorophores generated as byproducts during pyrolysis [34–36]. These 2-pyridone-based molecules can be floating independently in solution or can be attached to CQDs nanoparticles [37]. The effect of these fluorophores on catalytic activity, whether beneficial or not, remains to be clarified. Currently, most studies discussing CQDs deployment focus on their synthesis and characterization methods, occasionally touching upon their purification from carbonaceous impurities, but often neglecting to consider the impact of photoluminescent material present in CQDs solutions. Furthermore, many researchers reported their direct use without applying any post- synthesis treatment step. Thus, the separation of fluorophores from CQDs, and its subsequent impact on photocatalysts performance remain elusive and not well addressed in the literature.

Herein, we synthesized CQDs solutions by the microwave method from citric acid (CA) and urea (U) as precursors. The separation method adopted is column chromatography. The isolated fractions were tested separately by doping them on g-C3N4 and then evaluating the composite performance in MB degradation. The importance of the treatment of CQDs prior of utilization as well as the effect of heat treatment on Φ were demonstrated. Our findings clearly illustrate how the modification of the synthesis parameters as well as post-synthesis treatment can significantly influence the catalyst’s activity, the photoluminescence, and the Φ of CQDs. In addition, to determine its benign qualities, the toxicity of the CQDs solution was tested on Lens culinaris. The results were evaluated to assess any adverse effects on the growth and health of the plants. Knowledge of the influence of synthesis parameters and purification processes on CQDs properties enables the implementation of effective synthesis and treatment procedures. This, in turn, enhances the catalytic activity of various photocatalytic semiconductors and improves the efficiency of bioimaging applications. To the best of our knowledge, the findings of this work have not been previously discussed.

Citric acid (> 99.9 %), urea (> 99.5 %), melamine (> 99 %), ammonium nitrate (> 99 %), ethanolamine (98 %), isopropyl alcohol (>99.9 %), ascorbic acid (99 %), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (99 %) and MB (> 97 %); were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ultrapure pure water was used for all performed experiments.

1 g of citric acid and 3 g of urea were mixed and dissolved in 10 ml of deionized water for 30 min. Then, the mixture was transferred inside a household microwave oven and subjected to a 10 min of 700 W irradiation. These parameters were chosen because black color formation, indicating the successful synthesis of CQDs, occurs at this duration and power level. Later, the black material formed was removed and kept to cool down until reaching the ambient temperature. About 50 ml of deionized water was added to the material formed and then the solution was treated for 1 hour in ultrasonic bath to break down aggregates. At the end, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and the residue was excluded. Supernatant obtained was stored in the dark at 4 °C and denoted “Fresh CQDs”.

Fresh CQDs solution was further purified by column chromatography. A column containing silica gel (70–230 mesh) and a mobile phase of dichloromethane/methanol (6:4 v:v) were used for this operation. Subsequently, the absorbance of the elution was measured to facilitate its fractionation into distinct parts (Fig. S1). The elution was partitioned into many segments based on a clear gradient of a decreasing absorbance measured by UV–vis. Separated samples were stored in fridge for further studies.

A crucible containing melamine and closed by its lid was placed in oven and treated under a temperature of 550 °C for 3 hours with a ramping temperature of 1 °C/min. These parameters were optimized in our latest manuscript, where it was demonstrated that treating melamine at 550 °C resulted in an increased intensity of the yellowish color and more peaks in the C – N heterocycles region of the FTIR spectra [24]. After the treatment, the crucible was allowed to gradually cool down to the ambient temperature within the oven. At the end, a yellowish material was obtained which proves the formation of g-C3N4 [38]. The material was then well grinded with an agar mortar and stored under vacuum.

After determining the dry material weight of fresh CQDs solution purified by column chromatography, a specific volume of the solution (0.2, 0.5 and 1 wt%) was added on 5 g of g-C3N4 with 20 ml of ultrapure water. The mixture was then mixed well for 4 hours and then subjected to ultra-sonication for 1 hour. Then, the mixture was dried for overnight at 80 °C and the sample was denoted “CCH-CQDs/g-C3N4”. additionally, another sample was synthesized by using the fresh CQDs solution, however, this time the obtained powder was subjected to a heat treatment for 2 hours at 500 °C, this sample was named “HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4”. This temperature was chosen because higher temperatures could be too harsh on the material, potentially degrading its properties. Additionally, another sample in which Fresh CQDs solution was utilized, but no heat treatment was performed, sample is denoted “Fresh-CQDs/g-C3N4”.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR - TEM) was conducted using an EFTEM Jeol 2200 FS microscope, with an acceleration voltage of 200 keV. The TEM sample, dispersed in water at a concentration of 1 mg/ml, was drop-cast onto a TEM grid (Cu; 200 mesh; Formvar/carbon) and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The samples' FTIR spectra were assessed in KBr pellets utilizing a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer, adhering to specified parameters. This involved 64 scans over the wavelength range of 400 – 4000 cm−1, with a resolution set at 2 cm−1. X-ray diffractograms were recorded on a PANanalytical X′Pert [3] Powder instrument using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å, U = 40 kV, I = 30 mA) within a 2θ range of 5 – 50 ° with a step size of 0.04 °. Raman spectra were obtained using a Thermo Scientific DXR Raman Microscope equipped with a diode laser (excitation at 780 nm). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out using the Shamrock 300i spectrograph (Andor, Oxford Instruments) with an EMCCD camera (Newton 971, Andor, Oxford Instruments), and a standard He-Cd laser system at 325 nm was employed for excitation. In order to avoid UV modification of the sample, the excitation intensity was reduced to 2 mW. Additionally, the obtained spectra were corrected to system spectral sensitivity. Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) studies were conducted on a Setaram Labsys EVO device, with 10 mg of catalyst deposited in an alumina crucible and heated from ambient temperature to 800 °C (heating ramp 5 °C/min) under an air flow of 50 ml/min. The absorbance of the prepared materials was measured using an Agilent Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer. When it comes to electrochemical measurements, a conventional three-electrode configuration was employed to assess the conductivity and resistance of the materials under investigation. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted at a scan rate of 5 mV/s in a 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution. The counter and reference electrodes consisted of Pt and Ag/AgCl in a 3.5 M KCl solution, respectively. To prepare the working electrodes, 6 mg of freshly synthesized sample was mixed with 60 μl of a 5 wt% Nafion solution under ultrasonication. Subsequently, the resulting powder was dried at room temperature and applied onto the polished glassy carbon electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out at a bias potential of − 0.2 V. Data collection and processing were conducted using Thermo Scientific OMNIC 8.0 software (ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).

Photocatalytic testing involved the utilization of a 100 ml quartz double jacket reactor where MB (10 mg/l) served as the model contaminant compound. Initially, the photocatalyst was dispersed at a concentration of 1 g/l and subjected to mechanical stirring in the MB solution in complete darkness for a minimum of 2 h to achieve complete adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the suspension underwent vertical irradiation using a 300 W solar simulator (Asahi Spectra, model HAL-320) positioned at a distance to detect 1 sun (100 mW/cm2), simulating standard sunlight conditions to evaluate the performance of the system under realistic illumination. To ensure that the intensity is well set, we have used a Sun Checker CS- 20. At specified intervals, 2 ml samples were extracted, filtered at 0.2 µm, and analyzed through UV–vis spectrophotometry (Agilent CARY 5000). The absorption value at 665 nm was used to monitor the degradation kinetics, allowing the examination of the catalytic activity of the materials under investigation through reaction kinetics. At the end of each degradation experiment, the catalyst was filtered, washed with ethanol and dried at 80 °C.

The quantum yield (Φ) which is the ratio between the number of emitted to absorbed photons, was measured using two different methods. The first method is based on comparison of PL emission of the samples with PL response of Coumarin 120 (Φ ∼ 0.95, in methanol) and STB-3 (Φ ∼ 0.85, in methanol) which are a well-known dyes [39,40]. The Φ of the dyes were also verified by IS measurements. Whereas the second method is based on the absolute method in the integrating sphere (IS) setup.

When it comes to the first method, the PL of the samples and the dye were measured in water dispersions in quartz cuvettes having the same transmittance (∼ 93 %). The samples were excited using standard He-Cd laser at 325 nm using power of 0.5 mW. The PL was collected at a 90 ° angle using optical fiber and analyzed using Shamrock 300i spectrograph (Andor, Oxford Instruments) equipped with EMCCD camera (Newton 971, Andor, Oxford Instruments). Resulting spectra were corrected to sensitivity of the whole system and integrated. The Φ was then estimated following the equation below (Eq.2), which is based on an established procedure [41]:

Also, both used dyes were dispersed in methanol to keep comparable refractive index with water that was CQDs dispersant (n meot = 1.331, n water = 1.33).

In brief, the setup of the second method consists of the white-light excitation source EQ-99X (Energetiq) from which the excitation wavelength 360 nm was chosen by grating monochromator MSH 150 (LOT-Quantum design). The excitation light was passed into IS through the entry port by optical fiber. The samples in cuvettes were placed in IS in the direct path of the excitation beam. The light was collected from the exit port of the IS by another optical fiber and passed into the same analysis system as previous. The exit port was recessed into the perpendicular wall to the entry port to not have a direct line of sight on the sample or the entry port. The measured spectrum is then a result of multiple reflections from the highly reflective walls of the IS of the excitation beam and (potentially) PL from the sample. The Φ is then determined by comparing the measured spectra of sample in the cuvette and solvent in the cuvette according to the Eq.3 [42]:

Time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy was realized using streak camera Hamamatsu C10627 showing the best resolution of 15 ps. As an excitation light source, we utilized femtosecond laser PHAROS (150 fs pulses, 3 kHz repetition rate, Light Conversion) at 325 nm) to correspond to time-integrated PL measurements. The pulses at such frequency were generated by an optical parametric amplifier ORPHEUS (Light Conversion). All the spectra were corrected to spectral sensitivity of the system. The analysis of the data is based on the procedure outlined elsewhere [43,44]. In short, the PL decays as a function of wavelength are transformed to photon energies including the 1/λ2 correction factor. The decays are deconvoluted using Gaussian curve and PL decay function. The Gaussian curve representing the laser pulse as measured by the detection system or the instrumental response function. The decay curve was approximated by two exponential functions and the resulting amplitudes A1 (eV) and A2(eV) and time decay τ1(eV) and τ2(eV) constants are evaluated. The procedure to calculate the deviations of the parameters is presented in previous publication [44]. To obtain temporal decay constant independent on used mathematical model, we have also evaluated temporal PL decays of the samples using novel method involving calculation of average time constant of the decay using following procedure [44]:

The size distribution of synthesized materials was assessed, and results are in SI part (Fig. S2). The chemical structures of fresh CQDs and their tuned counterparts were determined by FTIR measurements, and results are shown in Fig. 1 a. Stretching vibrations of O – H, N – H and C – H are detected by absorption bands between 3500 cm−1 and 2800 cm−1 [45]. In particular, the band at 1718 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic stretching vibration of the carboxylic group C ═ O [46]. Furthermore, absorption bands are detected at 1400 cm−1 and 1220 cm−1, which corresponds to the bending vibrations of C – H and the stretching vibrations of the acyl group R – C ═ O, respectively [37]. When it comes to HTR CQDs and CCH CQDs, their FTIR display similarities. All CQDs samples were highly dispersible in water, which confirms their high possession of surface polar groups. On the other hand, the three samples, g-C3N4, HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 and CCH-CQDs/g-C3N4, display the same FTIR spectra with the typical bands attributable to the graphitic phase. The broad band between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O – H groups of absorbed H2O molecules and the – NH2 stretching vibration of uncondensed amine groups [47,48]. The stretching vibration modes of C – N, C ═ N and N – N bonds of heptazine and triazole are detected at 12,000 – 1700 cm−1, representing a high polycondensation degree [49,50]. The two bands at 1319 cm−1 and 1243 cm−1 correspond to C – NH – C connected units [51]. Moreover, the vibration band at 810 cm−1 is assigned to the out-of-plane breathing vibration of tri-s-triazine rings [52,53]. Doping with HTR-CQDs or CCH-CQDs did not modify the main structure of g-C3N4 owing to the low doping levels involved.

The phase structure of the materials in question was characterized by XRD. The XRD patterns in Fig. 1 b show a peak at 2θ = 27.62 ⁰ corresponding to the (002) crystal facets reflecting the interplanar stacking of the aromatic system in g-C3N4 [54,55]. It is noted that the introduction of CQDs did not alter the crystal structure of g-C3N4, however, a small shift in peak’s center is detected (2θ = 27.73 ⁰) which corresponds to a change in the interlayer arrangement of the aromatic structures towards a more compact configuration (Fig. S3) [56]. When it comes to CQDs, the tested fresh solution exhibited an amorphous character, which could be attributed to the abundance of non-crystalline entities present or the lower concentration of pure CQDs. Conversely, the fraction isolated by column chromatography (CCH CQDs), as well as the heat treated CQDs (HTR CQDs), both displayed a broad peak corresponding to the (002) planes of graphite centered at 26 ⁰ and 27.38 ⁰, respectively [57]. HTR-CQDs exhibiting a sharper peak than those of CCH- CQDs, is an indication of a higher crystallinity, indicative of a purer state with fewer non-crystalline elements present in the solution.

The synthesized materials were further characterized by Raman spectroscopy to investigate structural defects (Fig. 1 c). Under 780 nm laser excitation, g-C3N4 and HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 exhibited the characteristic vibrations at 472 cm−1, 701 cm−1, 741 cm−1, 980 cm−1 and 1236 cm−1 [58,59]. These peaks are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the aromatic C – N heterocycle, which is distinctive for melem [60,61]. When it comes to fresh CQDs, under 532 nm excitation, the spectra did not show any peak. Yang et al. explained the impossibility of obtaining the D and G band when using Raman to characterize the CQDs by the high fluorescence of the material [62]. Bourlinos et al. [63] and Wang et al. [64] reported similar results. However, both CCH-CQDs and HTR-CQDs showed the characteristic D and G bands of the carbon graphitic structure at 1318 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. The initial peak arises from the oscillations of sp3 hybrid carbons located in the terminal planes of disordered graphite, while the subsequent peak is attributed to the vibrations of sp2-bonded carbons in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [65,66]. Overall, Raman spectroscopy shows that the carbon-based fluorescent species were excluded by column chromatography, and they were potentially consumed under heat treatment.

To validate the composition of the fresh CQDs solution produced, additional characterizations were carried out. TGA was employed to determine the change in weight percentage after drying the CQDs solution. The analysis of TGA profile (Fig. 1 d) reveals the presence of three distinct stages identified by the difference in combustion temperature of different elements present in the carbonaceous mixture. The first stage corresponding to bound water evaporation and impurity degradation extends from ambient temperature to 250 °C, in which the carbon material underwent a 10 % weight loss. Subsequently, with a temperature range of 250 °C to 550 °C, there was an important 50 % loss of weight, corresponding to the continuing degradation of impurities. The last stage, corresponds to the full degradation at 700 °C. The identification of many degradation stages indicates the presence of distinctive species within CQDs with different thermal stability.

The insights in XRD and Raman were corroborated in UV–vis ( Fig. 2 a). Upon coupling with g-C3N4, the optical properties of CQDs are quenched. Therefore, optical characterizations were solely performed on CQDs rather than on the whole CQDs/g-C3N4 composite. The UV–vis profile of fresh CQDs solution shows the presence of two main regions of absorption: (1) n – π * (345 nm) and (2) π – π * (245 nm) transitions corresponding to CTA (citrazinic acid), HPPT (4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo(3,4-c)pyridine-1,3,6(2H,5H)-trione), and CYA (cyanuric acid) [67–69]. These species are known to be highly fluorescent and are denoted fluorophores. The remarkable decrease in the intensity of these regions of absorption in HTR-CQDs and in CCH-CQDs, along with the previous characterization results, shows that column chromatography was successful in isolating CQDs from free fluorophores. Furthermore, heat treatment proved to be efficient in producing a material without fluorophores.

To investigate the emission characteristics of our tuned CQDs products, we conducted an additional analysis of their PL properties. Fig. 2 b shows the PL spectra under 325 nm wavelength excitation. The freshly prepared CQDs solution exhibited the highest PL intensity, which can be attributed to the abundant presence of fluorophores within it. While the CCH-CQDs and HTR-CQDs showed a notable weakened emission peak intensity. The decrease in PL intensity as pyrolysis proceeds is an indication of consumption of fluorophores in the favor of CQDs, and the source of their remaining PL detected are their carbogenic cores. Krysmann et al. reported that the carbonization for prolonged periods leads to fluorophores crosslinking and the formation of sp3 hybridized CQDs [46]. For instance, the accumulation of CQDs is linked to significant cross-linking reactions and the creation of interchain amide bonds, as indicated by the peak centered at 1718 cm−1 (representing the stretching of C ═ O in the amide group) in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1 a). The formation of amide derivatives under microwave irradiation was reported by Seijas et al. [70]. The decrease in the fluorescence signal due to the reduction of the capacity of CQDs to emit light is referred to “quenching effect”. This observed quenching effect is an evident indication of the ability of heat treated CQDs fractions and purified ones with column chromatography to improve the separation of photo-induced charges and to thwart the rate of recombination. This could anticipate a superior photocatalytic performance after integration in the g-C3N4 semiconductor [71].

XPS findings (Fig. S4) validated the prevalence of fluorescent species containing carbonyl and amine functional groups in the Fresh CQDs solutions, along with the domination of carbon featuring pyridine, pyrrolic and amide structures in the HTR-CQDs solutions. These results align with observations made through UV–vis, and PL spectra.

The surface area of bare g-C3N4 and other doped materials was determined as shown in Table S1 in SI part. There is no significant change in the surface area after doping with CQDs, rather retain its own surface area. In the present study, modification of material induces mainly chemical structure changes rather than physical changes which makes them outstanding material to enhance electrical conductivity thereby activity. In fact, all samples had a low surface area in the range of 7–17 m2/g.

To obtain further conclusive evidence on the charge transfer characteristics and the charge recombination process of g-C3N4, CCH-CQDs/g-C3N4 and HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were performed (Fig. S5a). Materials exhibiting smaller arc radii in the EIS demonstrate superior charge carrier migration, enhanced conductivity, and reduced electron-hole pair recombination rates [72]. In the high-frequency region of the EIS plot, the observed semicircular curve corresponds to charge transfer resistance. Among the three tested samples, the arc radius follows this sequence: g-C3N4 > CCH-CQDs/g-C3N4 > HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4. The notably smaller arc radius of the HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 sample indicates superior conductivity, more efficient charge transport, and reduced resistance. Fig. S5b further displays the electrostatic polarization curves for g-C3N4, CCH-CQDs/g-C3N4, and HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4. The HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 sample shows a current density three times greater than that of unmodified g-C3N4, reflecting improved separation and movement of photo-generated charge carriers under light, compared to the other materials. Consequently, this result further corroborates the EIS findings that the thermal treatment of CQDs enhances the electrochemical properties of g-C3N4.

The morphological structures of the produced CQDs and the synthesized g-C3N4 and HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 were examined by TEM. Fig. 3 a clearly shows the CQDs with a diameter ranging between 2 and 3.5 nm. The synthesized g-C3N4 (Fig. 3 b) appeared as the typical layered flakes-like structure, which is in accordance with previous studies [73]. Both CCH-CQDs/g-C3N4 and HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 samples were also visualized (Fig. 3 c & Fig. 3 d), herein, the fundamental structure of the original g-C3N4 remained unchanged, the unexpected detection of the CQDs by TEM was not foreseen, likely due to its low loading (0.5 wt%). The difficulty in detecting CQDs on the catalyst's surface might be due to the following reasons: First, the carbon material may be effectively dispersed in the solution because of oxygen and nitrogen groups on its surface, which improve its stability and integration with g-C3N4. Second, after extensive ultrasonic treatment and prolonged mixing, the CQDs may become embedded within the core structure of the g-C3N4 rather than just remaining on the surface. These factors make it challenging to observe the CQDs directly on the catalyst's surface. This finding supports the notion that proximity between CQDs and g- C3N4 could facilitate strong interfacial charge transfer, leading to better photocatalytic activity. Gu et al. described similar outcomes in which the carbon material was not visible on the surface of g-C3N4 [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence and quantum yield evaluation of CQDs solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The measurements conducted to ascertain the PL intensity were performed using liquids with comparable absorption properties. This approach ensures that the intensities obtained can be compared directly, without being influenced by variations in the amount of absorbed light. The PL intensity was assessed by integrating the signal across the entire spectral band, providing a comprehensive evaluation of the emitted light's intensity (Fig. S6). The influence of many parameters on PL was assessed, including the effect of nitrogen source, the effect of citric acid to urea ratio (CA/U), the effect of irradiation duration, and the influence of the microwave power.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nitrogen source on PL
ce:section.ce:para: The choice of nitrogen source has an influence of CQDs optical properties. When urea is used as the source of nitrogen, CQDs solution displayed the highest PL intensity, while melamine exhibited the lowest ( Fig. 4 a). Furthermore, UV–vis profiles in Fig. 4 b revealed that the choice of nitrogen source significantly affects the absorbance characteristics of the material, with a main peak centered at 350 nm and a shoulder at 250 nm. Specifically, when urea is used as nitrogen source, the CQDs solution exhibited the highest absorbance, compared with ammonium nitrate and melamine which displayed lower values. Simultaneously, when urea and ethanolamine are used, the resulting solution is dark black, however, the two other precursors gave brighter colors as shown in Fig. S7. The variance in PL exhibited by CQDs solutions synthesized from different nitrogen sources as precursors can be explained by the fact that different nitrogen sources contribute distinct functional groups and heteroatoms, which influence the electronic structure and surface chemistry of the resulting CQDs [75]. For instance, nitrogen doping plays a pivotal role in modulating the density and distribution of defect states within the CQD lattice [76], affecting the recombination dynamics of photoexcited charge carriers, and subsequently impacting the PL characteristics [77].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CA/U ratio on PL
ce:section.ce:para: Many authors discussed CQDs synthesis with some specific CA/U ratio but without studying the effect of this ratio variation. Our experimentations showed that the solutions synthesized with increasing amounts of urea exhibited a correspondingly higher PL intensity, reaching its peak at 1/4 ratio. However, beyond this ratio, the PL intensity declined sharply, reaching a minimum at a 1/20 ratio as shown in Fig. 5 a. These PL results were consistent with UV–vis profile (Fig. 5 b), where 1/4 sample showed the highest absorbance at 410 nm, while the 1/20 exhibited the lowest absorbance at this wavelength. It can be inferred from these results that optimal production of fluorophores occurs at a 1/4 ratio of CA to urea. Initially, as the concentration of urea is increased, there is a proportional enhancement in the nitrogen doping level within the CQDs, which facilitates the radiative recombination of photoexcited charge carriers, hence augmenting the PL intensity. However, beyond an optimal urea concentration, several factors come into play that can lead to a decline in the PL intensity. In fact, excessive urea concentrations may result in the formation of larger CQDs or aggregation/agglomeration of CQD clusters because of increased precursor concentration, leading to a reduction in surface-to-volume ratio. The latter can lead to an increased non-radiative recombination pathway causing a suppression in emission. Additionally, higher urea concentrations might promote the formation of unwanted byproducts or side reactions during the synthesis, such as carbonaceous impurities acting as surface quenchers, which can impact the PL properties of CQDs solutions [78].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of microwave irradiation duration on PL
ce:section.ce:para: In terms of treatment duration, there is an observed trend of increasing PL intensity with longer treatment periods, reaching an optimal intensity at 16 minutes, as shown in Fig. 6 a. However, beyond this optimal treatment time, the PL intensity begins to decline. Similarly, UV–vis analysis (Fig. 6 b) exhibited the highest absorbance for sample treated for 16 minutes, but absorbance decreased with longer microwave exposures. The increase in PL of CQDs and fluorophores solutions with longer microwave irradiation time up to a maximum, followed by a decrease with prolonged irradiation, can be attributed to the dynamic evolution of CQD formation, structural transformation, and surface passivation processes during microwave-assisted synthesis [79]. Initially, with longer microwave irradiation times, there is an extended duration for the nucleation and growth of CQDs and fluorophores from the precursor molecules (Fig. S8). This prolonged exposure allows enhanced precursor decomposition, leading to the formation of a greater number of CQDs and fluorophores. The increased amount of CQDs and fluorophores contributes to a higher PL intensity because of the greater number of emissive sites and increased radiative recombination pathways available within the material (Fig. S9). Furthermore, longer irradiation times can facilitate the completion of surface passivation processes, wherein functional groups from precursor molecules or the surrounding environment are effectively anchored onto the CQD surface, reducing surface defects, and enhancing radiative recombination efficiency. This surface passivation enhances the Φ of PL emission, further augmenting the observed PL intensity. However, beyond an optimal irradiation time, an overgrowth or aggregation of CQDs can take place, leading to the formation of larger CQD clusters or aggregates with reduced surface-to-volume ratios [80]. Additionally, prolonged irradiation times may lead to the degradation of fluorophores, which suppresses their emission. Furthermore, prolonged durations can lead to the formation of carbonaceous impurities, surface quenchers, or non-emissive species, which compromise the PL properties of the mixture [81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of applied microwave power on PL
ce:section.ce:para: For the same parameters, the power of microwave radiation emerged as a pivotal factor influencing the optical properties of CQDs solutions. For instance, the PL intensity of the produced CQDs solution was too low when the synthesis was performed under 100 W. However, a sharp rise in PL intensity is observed with the increase in microwave power, quickly culminating in a plateau at 450 W ( Fig. 7 a). Simultaneously, as shown in Fig. 7 b, UV–vis profiles showed that the absorbance of the solutions was higher when the microwave power was higher during synthesis. Initially, the application of higher microwave power leads to more rapid heating of the reaction mixture, accelerating the precursor decomposition process and the polymerization of CQDs and organic fluorophores [82]. This accelerated decomposition results in increased generation of reactive species and higher local concentrations of carbonaceous intermediates, promoting the nucleation and growth of CQDs. Consequently, higher PL intensity is observed as a greater number of CQDs and emissive fluorophores are formed within the solution. However, beyond the 450 W microwave power threshold, the PL intensity may reach a plateau where further increase in power do not lead to a significant enhancement in PL. At this point, the reaction system may reach a state of equilibrium in which the rate of CQDs and fluorophores formation becomes saturated, and the additional energy input does not result in substantial changes in the overall properties of the mixture and the behaviour of CQDs and fluorophores. This could explain the adoption of power intensities between 400 and 700 W by most of the authors in the literature.

The significant (PL) observed suggests an elevated presence of either free or anchored fluorophores as well as CQDs. Fluorophores offer valuable utility in imaging applications, while CQDs show promise in photocatalysis endeavors. When characterization results and PL study are combined, it can be understood that under mild synthesis conditions, fluorophores dominate. However, under prolonged microwave irradiation exposure (20 min at 600 W) or in the case of post-synthesis pyrolysis (250 ⁰ C), fluorophores are consumed while an enhanced build-up of the carbogenic core of CQDs takes place. To gain deeper insight into the effect of synthesize conditions on CQDs solutions properties, the Φ was assessed.

Results of Φ measurements are listed in Table1. It was observed that the Φ of samples measured by IS method are two-fold higher than those calculated by the comparative method. This difference can be explained by the effect of light scattering. In the comparative method, the Φ for sample B was determined to be 9.5 %, and for sample C, 8.7 %, with sample A estimated at approximately 0.35 % based on its low PL (for more detail and representative PL measurements, see Fig. S10a and Fig. S10b in supplementary information). However, the IS method yielded significantly higher Φ values for samples B and C, at 17.6 % and 20.8 %, respectively, while the Φ of sample A was too low to measure (for representative PL measurements, see Fig. S11). This difference arises because the comparative method, which relied on comparing the PL intensity of the sample to used dyes. In theory, to apply the comparative method correctly, we should know the absorption of the samples, however it is commonly calculated using dispersion transmittance. This is correct way to calculate absorption when scattering effects are weak. However, this assumption is not valid while the dispersion contains small particles such as QDs that camouflage the real absorption of the sample and brings high error in absorption determination.Würth et al. reached similar conclusions, and stated that scattering can cause a substantial loss of detected light, leading to an underestimation of the true Φ [83]. In contrast, the IS method captures all emitted light, including scattered light, providing a more accurate measurement of the true Φ. The significant difference in Φ values between the two methods highlights the importance of using techniques like the IS that can fully account for scattering when measuring the Φ of nanoparticle solutions.

The observed increase in Φ with extended treatment time between 4 and 16 minutes, with the most significant improvement occurring at 8 minutes, suggests a progressive enhancement in the photoluminescence efficiency of the CQDs as the treatment proceeds. This increase in Φ can be attributed to several factors, including the improvement in surface passivation [75], and the reduction of non-radiative recombination sites [84]. As treatment time increases, the surface defects, which often act as non-radiative pathways that quench fluorescence, are likely reduced, leading to more efficient photon emission and, consequently, a higher Φ [85]. However, the relatively small difference in Φ between the 8 and 16 minutes treatments indicates that a plateau might be reached around 8 minutes. This shows that while extended treatment times can enhance Φ by improving CQDs quality, there is a limit to the benefits, beyond which additional treatment time does not lead to an important increase.

To elucidate the processes behind the observed influence of pyrolysis time during the synthesis of CQDs on their QY value (Table 1), we have analyzed emission dynamics of these samples using time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL). The TRPL measurements were performed with a streak camera. To ensure comparable experimental conditions with already presented time-integrated PL characterization the samples were exited using femtosecond pulses at 325 nm. The resulting 2D maps of the spectral and temporal PL dependence of samples A, B, C are presented in (Fig. S12).

Firstly, the observed spectra closely match PL spectra obtained using time-integrated measurements ( Fig. 8 a). The slight red shift of their maxima can be attributed to differences in energy state filling mechanisms when laser pulses are involved.

Secondly, it is evident that the PL decays of all samples occur within a nanosecond temporal window, typical for allowed transitions (Fig. 8 b), as opposed to excitonic or surface trap radiative recombination processes. The latter dynamics are common in semiconductor nanoparticles/nanostructures, which are typically observed in microsecond/millisecond temporal intervals [44,86]. Interestingly, the PL decays of all samples exhibit similar behavior (Fig. 8 b). These dynamics cannot be approximated solely by a single-exponential curve but can be well-fitted using two-exponential or stretched-exponential curves. This behavior is characteristic of particle ensembles with a continuous variation of lifetimes across particles [44].

To quantify the observed dynamics, we applied a two-exponential model and evaluated the spectral dependence both time constants and their amplitudes (Figure S13). Although the use of a two-exponential fit is a standard procedure, while we do not expect the presence of two distinct radiative recombination channels, we prefer a novel method to quantify PL dynamics of particle ensembles showing such behavior. To obtain parameter that could be used to characterize temporal evolution of the decay independently on the used mathematical model, we calculated the spectral dependence of so-called PL average time constant τAV (λ), which can be considered as the mean lifetime of the PL (Fig. 8 c). Based on our calculations, the τAV (λ) of all our samples exhibited non-trivial spectral behavior, with maximum values of 9.4 ns, 9.2 ns, and 7.4 ns for samples A, B, and C, respectively. The observed variation of τAV (λ) values within the samples can account for the difference in QY between samples B and C, but it does not explain the minimal QY of sample A. Within the framework of these findings, the evolution of QY of the CQD ensembles with pyrolysis treatment intervals ranging from 4 to 12 minutes aligns with the discussion derived from time-integrated luminescence measurements and can be interpreted as follows:

The comparable PL dynamics of samples A and B (or C), combined with sample A's 1000-fold weaker integrated intensity, indicate an extremely high concentration of dark CQDs in this sample. Such particles absorb light but do not exhibit PL due to highly effective non-radiative recombination processes. While PL measurements can only detect the response of the emitting particles, the overall ensemble is characterized by the light from only a few emitting particles, resulting in an extremely low QY for the ensemble as a whole. Extending the pyrolysis time from 4 to 8 minutes likely quenching the most effective non-radiative pathways and thus activates the dark particles, possibly by saturating surface defects and improving structural quality, leading to a significant enhancement of the ensemble QY. Further increasing of treatment time to 12 minutes does not result in additional particle activation but instead only decrease the efficiency of the persistent non-radiative processes, thereby increasing the QY proportionally to the increase of τAV (λ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs application: MB degradation by CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal loading of CQDs was determined by running photodegradation experiments and results are present in the supporting information part (Fig. S14). A catalyst concentration of 1 g/l was adopted in all experiments. When integrated in g-C3N4, both CCH-CQDs/g-C3N4 and HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 showed an improvement in activity compared to the case where fresh CQDs are doped in g-C3N4 ( Fig. 9 a). For instance, a 90 % MB degradation was achieved in 3 hours in the case of HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4, while Fresh- CQDs did not show any improvement compared to the bare g-C3N4 catalyst (58 %). Another experiment was performed in which the first elution part of column chromatography believed to contain the fluorophores was used to synthesize a fluorophores/g-C3N4 catalyst. Herein, the doping did not show any improvement; on the contrary, it led to a decrease in the activity of bare g-C3N4. When combining the activity results, together with the characterizations (Raman, UV–vis, and PL), it becomes clear that fluorophores do not play a role in catalysis and both column chromatography and heat treatment were successful in degrading them.

The stability of HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 was investigated by running three consecutive MB degradation experiments under the same optimized experimental conditions (Fig. 9 b). Following each run, the catalyst was rinsed with methanol and dried at 80 °C overnight. The catalyst exhibited a high and stable degradation capability of 90 %, showing the high potential of this material to be adopted in large-scale operations. To understand the increased activity of HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 in contrast to g-C3N4, the underlying mechanism was investigated. Comparison to materials in literature is provided in Table S2, which shows the competitiveness of the synthesized material HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4.

Reactive species trapping experiments were conducted to elucidate the degradation mechanism of MB on HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 ( Fig. 10). Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ascorbic acid, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were used as scavengers for hydroxyl radicals (• OH), superoxide radicals (•
O
2
−
), and holes (h+), respectively [87]. The results reveal a marginal decrease in activity with EDTA, suggesting a minor role for holes in MB degradation, while significant reductions in activity (approximately 60 % and 56 % for hydroxyl and superoxide trapping, respectively) on the composite surface after inhibition of other species underscore their crucial involvement. This observation demonstrates that hydroxyl radicals (• OH) and superoxide radicals (•
O
2
−
) are accountable for MB photodegradation on our material surface.

When a semiconductor is exposed to light with photon energy surpassing its bandgap energy, excited electrons migrate from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), generating electron/hole pairs. CB-excited electrons are responsible for reduction reactions, while VB holes drive oxidation reactions. However, the excited electrons can predominantly recombine with the holes without engaging in reactions, which decreases the overall efficiency of the catalyst. On HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 surface, the integrated CQDs extracts electrons from the CB of the catalyst (− 1.03 V vs (NHE)) [88], thus prolonging the lifetime of charge carriers. This hypothesis is supported by PL measurements in which HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 showed the lowest PL, which is an indicator of a lower recombination rate (Fig. 2 b). The captured electrons can then react with dissolved oxygen in water on the composite surface to generate superoxide species (− 0.33 V vs. NHE) [89], which subsequently react with H+ to produce hydroxyl radicals through the formation of hydrogen peroxide (+ 0.68 V vs. NHE) [90], owing to its efficient conductivity, as confirmed by electrochemical measurements (Fig. S5). The direct formation of hydroxyl radicals from water (H2O/• OH: + 2.4 V vs. NHE) and OH ‾/• OH (1.99 V) is improbable on the composite surface due to the lower oxidation potential of VB of HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 (+ 1.60 V vs. NHE) [27]. The proficient electron trapping nature of CQDs within the composite is pivotal for electron-mediated reduction reactions. The CQDs material is incorporated in g-C3N4 by carbon-bridging. The carbon-bridging modification improves the original conjugate system and optimizes the band structure of g-C3N4, as well as causing charge redistribution, resulting in improved electron-hole separation efficiency and visible light utilization rate.

The toxicity of CQDs and the catalyst was independently studied on Lens culinaris seeds in vitro over a period of 14 days ( Fig. 11). Diluted solutions of fresh CQDs, CCH CQDs, and HTR CQDs with same concentrations that match the amount of CQDs present in our synthesized 0.5CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst (1 g/l) were used as a growth medium. Simultaneously, fresh water was used as the growth medium for another experiment serving as a control. The seeds grown in fresh CQDs clearly exhibited a faster growth rate, reaching a 15 cm elongation in 14 days of cultivation. On the other hand, seeds cultivated in other mediums showed a slower growth rate of 5 cm elongation in a 14-day period. The reason behind the enhanced growth in the case of fresh CQDs could be the presence of nitrogenous species and impurities that served as a fertilizer that boosted the germination process. In fact, before purification, the fresh CQDs solution exhibits characteristics like a fertilizer, primarily due to its nitrogen content and other nutrient-rich species originating from the urea and citric acid precursors. Urea, a nitrogen-rich compound, introduces nitrogen into the CQDs in the form of amine and amide groups, which are known to support plant growth by promoting chlorophyll production and protein synthesis critical for overall plant development. Nitrogen, as an essential macronutrient, plays a key role in these biological processes [91]. Additionally, citric acid contributes carbon and other potential beneficial elements, such as trace organics that might enhance microbial activity in the soil, indirectly supporting nutrient availability and uptake [92]. However, the purified samples “CCH CQDs” and “HTR CQDs” showed a similar growth rate to that of the control samples. These results unambiguously suggest the biocompatibility of CQDs. Furthermore, the synthesized g-C3N4 composite was tested for its toxicity and did not show an effect on growth rate. Additionally, under 365 nm UV light excitation, the healthy seedlings cultivated in the CQDs solution exhibited photoluminescence (Fig. 11 b), providing concrete evidence of the harmless existence of CQDs within their physiological tissues.

This study demonstrates the significant role of CQD constituent purification in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, particularly in methylene blue (MB) degradation. The efficient microwave synthesis of CQDs, combined with post-synthesis purification via column chromatography or heat treatment, effectively improved the photoactivity of CQDs/g-C3N4 composites. Column chromatography successfully isolated fluorophores, enhancing photocatalytic efficiency, while heat treatment facilitated the conversion of fluorescent byproducts into carbogenic cores, increasing quantum yield and photocatalytic activity. The findings underscore the critical impact of post-synthesis purification and synthesis parameter control on CQD properties, providing insights for optimizing CQDs for specific applications, including biomedical use. The HTR-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst's high activity, durability, and demonstrated safety highlight its practical potential in photocatalysis and other sustainable applications.
